item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good but doesn't compare to the original. This one feels bloated at times and sometimes it feels just a tad bit too connected to your other stories. I know you said that it isn't a rewrite and is it's own story, but I came here for the original and was slightly disappointed at what I got. Like another commenter said above, I'm not a big fan of huge apocalyptic scenarios just kinda ruining the shared universe without fixing it at the end, especially if it's just a regular ole numbered SCP, but that's just me and I completely respect your opinion if you feel different. All in all though, I feel unsatisfied at the end and annoyed that I have to read the rest of the mythos to fully understand it. I like simple stuff that can tell a story on it's own, which is still possible if you add references to other articles, but this one doesn't hit it for me.

Anyone know where I can read the original article?

item.title: something just occured to me
item.content:encoded: it could be that the two stars shown in the picture aren't the only stars left in existence, but mere the only stars left who haven't covered themselves with a light-obscuring thaumaturgic plasma veil. after all if we can't see them through ours, chances are that they can't see us through ours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be an AMAZING name for a band, even by the standards of "great name for a band" type phrases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ultimate Sunbro. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
> WOULD YOU LIKE TO VOTE BEFORE LOGGING OUT?
no

What a mad lad

More seriously, it's an upvote from. I really like the concept. + It reminds me of SCP-179

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While individual articles can be accepted or ignored as canon, the basic conceit is that any of them COULD exist in conjunction with any other. One SCP shouldn't deliberately contravene or contradict another.

With thousands of articles, this would be pretty much impossible. Trying to force perfect internal cohesion on a site where anybody can post any kind of article would be beyond difficult.

Moreover, having to stick to a single cohesive canon in every article seems like something that would make reading the site pretty stale, and more importantly, make writing for the site a tremendous task, as you would have to know everything that has been done in order to not contradict anything. The fun part of the wiki being collaborative is that anyone can just come in and write anything they like, which can build on existing materials, reinterpret them in new ways, or write something completely new. If there's something you don't like, then you can go and write a better version of it. If there's something you like, then you can freely expand on it in whatever way you want. Within a perfectly cohesive wiki, these wouldn't be possible, and I feel we would be overall worse off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yo, what are you talking about. There are more articles than this one that end the world. Hell, there are articles that straight up contradict each other and don't even have to end the world. If you take every single article as being canon and being in the same universe, you will run into serious problems about halfway through Series II.

This isn't a shared STORY. When you make an article, you aren't making an entry into one overarching story. When you make an article, you can pick and choose what you want to consider as canon. That's the collab.

The SCP format is just a challenge to writing a story. It isn't "not" a story, it's just a very different way of doing so. I don't see why it would be a problem that this story is being considered a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I understand the flexible canon angle, the underlying concept of the site is that the Foundation monitors and contains these anomalies. It's not about multiple parallel universes. They are interconnected. While individual articles can be accepted or ignored as canon, the basic conceit is that any of them COULD exist in conjunction with any other. One SCP shouldn't deliberately contravene or contradict another.
In fact, the entire SCP 001 section is based on the idea that only one of those is correct, but we aren't being told which one.
Accepting this as canon would by definition mean that the SCP site itself is now pointless, and there was no point reading any other article. There's no more concealment or containment becuase the universe is gone and the Earth is on it's way out as well.
For me, the greatest thing about a collaborative universe isn't to "take what you want and ignore the rest" to collaborate and work within that universes consistency. Writing a story that tramples over everything else and breaks the fundamental mythos isn't really making something as part of a collaborative universe. It's destroying that universe. Much harder is to integrate your ideas into the overarching mythos and have it all fit together, not fundamentally break it. THAT'S writing in a collaborative universe. Anyone trying to submit a story like this to a shared universe style anthology would be rejected out of hand for breaking the universe.
There's a difference between flexible canon that allows one to accept or ignore an article versus deliberately writing a Niven style "Down in Flames" (http://www.larryniven.net/stories/downinflames.shtml)

The other major thing that kills it for me is that this is more of a "story" than an analytical entry. The first half describes SCP-1548 and talks about containment and the like. And that's fine. But then the second half splits off into a creative writing exercise that really would do better as a Tale. Compare it to other SCP's where short stories relating to the anomaly are partitioned off as separate links, articles or addendums. They aren't part of the actual entry and can be included or ignored at will. Instead. this one rapidly loses the clinical and analytical tone of a database entry and swerves off into a space opera.
I can appreciate that this isn't the only entry that "breaks the world". I'm not a fan of that and feel it invalidates an article from being a part of the greater whole. Equally as important, the second half of this completely abandons the pretense of an encyclopedic entry in favour of overt dramatics.
Thanks for clarifying the sites canon guidelines though. This one for me is not only definitely non-canon, but is actually a mis-filed "Tale".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. And this is the greatest thing when you have a collaborative universe : you can take what you want and ignore what you don't like. ^^

(oh, and hello, Crayne, good to see you back)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You indeed missed something. ;) While this is a collaborative universe, everything AND nothing is canon. If you don't like SCP-1548, don't accept it as canon. There are more articles like this one, and downvoting them on the basis of a misunderstanding of the workings of this wiki would be a shame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1 Downvoted
I’m new around here so forgive me if I’ve missed something but for me this violates one of the most fundamental rules of writing in a shared collaborative universe - don’t wreck it for everyone else.
This isn’t an article it’s an Armageddon story that takes more than a decade of collaborative work by thousands of people and just says “Nope, stuff that, I’m burning it all to the ground.” Its the equivalent of kids playing cops and robbers and one of them saying “I dropped a bomb and everyone’s dead now. Games Over.”
There are plenty of articles here with the potential to end the world, but this one actually does it, so the rest of the SCP Foundation content is now pretty much irrelevant, if this was to be accepted as canon.
Move it to a speculative fiction site or something. One single SCP shouldn’t break every other one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't care for the entire Second Hytoth/Ortothan mythos as a whole (in fact, I've imagined more than once an incredibly petty tale where the cultists, intergalactically, are explicitly shown to be 100% wrong in their interpretations, being effectively duped into doing something else's dirty work), even if I discovered through this article's crosslinks, but fuck me did I enjoy this.

Doomsday skips of great scale are the sort of thing which either succeeds fantastically or crash and burn. While this particular scenario of universal annihilation is not the most original thing ever, either here on the wiki or elsewhere, it's presented magnificently. Each of the events in the log helps getting the utter scale of this through, and then the imagery of the space battles is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really not a fan. Ignoring the fact that I really liked 1548 and miss it, these epic scale SCPs strain my suspension of disbelief. This isn't a world which could be our own and at this point I dont really understand what the point of the foundation is. They're protecting something which doesn't actually exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, I really do.

I just don't like how wordy it is and how heavily it relies on other SCPs. Rakmou-leusan, for example, isn't even mentioned as an SCP, but I found out in the comments that it was a reference I was just supposed to get. And the Ortothans are another whole new mythos - I'd be more invested if I'd read the lengthy story behind them, which I'd rather not do.

Personally I also don't like SCPs that break what little canon or immersion there is (might as well make a tale, really), and I feel like this one is blatantly unfinished, like a story that ends halfway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t understand it, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lol, yeah. It's just like the sun is saying "oh what kind of fuckery is this" and just yeeting the threat out of the system.
Also, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a simple man. Ortothan plus space equals +1. All the rest makes me wish I could do a +1000 instead. A good read, all in all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow!! Your execution of this idea was incredible! The atmosphere and tone is phenomenal. Although it’s a bit lengthy, it’s a worth while read! +1

item.title: Overall +1, with 2 nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
First off, the technobabble in this isn't great:

tachyons moving at undetermined faster-than-light speeds

correct me if I'm wrong, but don't tachyons have to travel at FTL speeds?

Foundation satellites detect a sharp increase in thaumaturgic particles in orbit

This sounds a bit to much likes it's something from a bad star trek episode. "Captain, we're picking up a high concentration of Thaumaturgic particles." "Hit them with a phase enhanced quantum energy beam."

Finally, I think having the veil get lifted, suppressed, and then possibly lifted again, feels a bit awkward.

Still, overall solid piece that give a sense of epic scope and a dark atmosphere, it feels a lot like a climatic doctor who episode. I especially enjoyed that despite hardly having a happy ending, it also doesn't end with us concluding the world is definitely ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been mentioned in this very thread multiple times but the original author deleted it when he left the site. 9Volt and Varaxous decided to post their completely unrelated draft into the vacant slot, because someone had to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, I'm confused. What exactly happened to the original SCP-1548 article, and why has it been replaced?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, suspension of disbelief is an incredibly important part of any piece of fiction, and I know other people on this site disagree, but that's just something I feel is important. The entire reason Series 1 was so popular was because the writers tried hard to make their articles feel convincing, to write them in a world where the SCP Foundation COULD be our world. Certain articles broke that from time to time, and yes, I enjoyed them less because of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To add onto what Varaxous said, here are a handful of articles in which the world comes to an end:

  • Allison Eckhart (much less obvious than the others, but Addendum Allison Eckhart-2 does imply an apocalypse in progress)
  • SCP-3002
  • SCP-3449
  • SCP-3519
  • SCP-3570
  • SCP-3731
  • SCP-3733 (the world here isn't "over" but humanity as we know it essentially is)
  • SCP-4005

There's even a whole contest about ending the world. SCP-1548 far from new in doing this. The wiki is less about "how well could this fit into the real world" and more about writing in a shared continuity. I can understand disliking the apocalypse here for other reasons, but the reasoning being "this couldn't exist in the real world" doesn't make sense when that hasn't been a requirement for wiki content for a long while. This is also excluding the many articles like SCP-4427 and SCP-3109 which exist in versions of the world that are very different than the real world.

So no, if SCP-682 or any SCP had a line like that and have it actually work in the context of its narrative I wouldn't call bullshit. Narrative quality is the driving force behind the anomalies people write, not fitting thresholds for potentially existing in the real world. If a narrative can't fit in the real world then so be it. After all, as far as the wiki goes, there is no canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would this work as a tale and what would the difference be from this and an ‘alternate heading?’

I get not being sold on it but your reasons for not being sold on it make no sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… It destroyed the universe? Shouldn't SCP articles at least TRY to fit the narrative of, I dunno, the Earth in general? I think I would notice if Pluto got nuked. This would work better as a tale, or even an alternate SCP article under some other heading, but as it stands, I'm gonna downvote vehemently. Imagine if SCP-682's article read, "Scp-682 has killed many high-importance and high-profile individuals: including SCP-078, Donald Trump, The Queen of england, all of Australia, etc"

You'd call bullshit.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TS-1817-V7: Known as the 12 Holy Owls of Serrinithium; origin among humans unknown

TS-1817 originates from Ortothan occultists in the 1800s

So, is it just variant 7 of the symbol which has an unknown origin? I feel the need for clarification.

I don't love the idea of virtually the whole universe being destroyed as just the kicker at the end of an article. A threat that makes the sun fearful is frightnening enough, but I find horror more effective when there's still a thread of hope, though a fragile one. To me, the hopelessness of a destroyed universe just takes the emotional impact of this past fear into resignation.

…so I'll just ignore that part, because the rest of this was towering, and a thrill to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the most engrossing SCPs I've read. It's deep and long, but retains its coherence throughout. I was initially turned off by the references to magic and psionics, but the depth (and consistency) of the lore references persuaded me even before I dug into the logs.

It helps that this replaced the slot of one of my least favorite articles (I appreciate that some folks were fond of the hateful star, but I always found that article simplistic and unimaginative).

This is truly a stellar addition. Well done!

item.title: This is not a rewrite.
item.content:encoded:
And as I came into this blind I thoroughly enjoyed it.
It had me thinking about Kardashev scales, Simulation Theory and good old fashioned Elder Gods.
The universe is destroyed every other SCP, that's part of the fun here for me.

item.title: There is no canon
item.content:encoded: Seriously, once people recognise this their enjoyment of this site goes way up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This must be the work of an enemy STAND!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes me feel more accomplished as a coauthor than any +100 rating ever could.

Especially this. Seeing this made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So within the span of nearly one month, the Voruteut managed to destroy most of the universe. That might be a fun addition to the VS Battles Wiki once we get more information on them since the sun manages to destroy one of its troops.

https://vsbattles.wikia.com/wiki/SCP-1548

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh oh oh OH.

that makes much more sense.

ok, ty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It looks like there's a misunderstanding here. The author of the current 1548 did not remove the previous one. This page is an entirely new article, not a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the current authors can't really control whether the old author takes down the former one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The better idea would've been to add this to Series 4/5 instead of just removing one of the more iconic SCPs directly from the site.

Even that was a poor move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It fires solar lasers at Eldritch space monsters and creates a protective sheath of volitale plasma/Thaumaturgic energy. That is the anomaly.

As for big and bulky, yeah I can get that. Trying to juggle anomaly/ending universe/mythos building and having it work out for everyone is hard, but I don’t care what ‘the best SCP is.’ This is our SCP, and we wanted it to be big and important.

Sorry it wasn’t able to catch your interest as much as the original, even if the two are wholly unrelated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big, bulky, boring, hard to understand, doesn't grab my attention at all, I still have no idea how it is anomalous other than it has solar properties

The best SCPs are the ones that are not big and important. They're the ones that are rich in story and idea, are able to explain to even a new reader what is going on, and how it's anomaly functions.

The old one did all of this and more, including giving a speculation vibe on what will happen next to humanity.

This barely stands up to its predecessor. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, even if I didn't totally understand it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is… big. Wow. It's almost too big, but by the time everything finally clicked even without getting the underlying mythology I had to +1.

I was expecting extra-universal eaters but after all the vivid descriptions that picture is what finally hammered that there was nothing left. Nothing. And I don't think we'd want to be in the middle of the fight between the giant eel and our big, angry brother.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got it the first time around ^__^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops wrong ting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author of the original 1548 left and took it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…I'm gonna need a Leak thread post to understand this one… so an external force destroyed almost the entire universe except our star system and two others? And the Sun is actively fighting back?
Or is it the other way round?

Unrelated, why was the original 1548 deleted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I barely ever comment.
But this was amazing, I honestly mean it. It feels like one-hundred different threats thrown into one and I can’t be any happier. The fact that humanity allowed one of these creatures/things into the solar system and our own sun basically went “oh for goodness sake, I’ll deal with this”.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are destroying the universe for what? Because you can?

Yes.

Why write anything for the wiki? Why bother to make these stories?

We get paid nothing. While there are some very few exceptions, at the end of the day, the only benefit we (the writers) get are internet votes and some recognition.

The reason I wrote this with 9Volt? Because I could. There really doesn’t need to be a greater reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We had an entire contest about doomsday scenarios.

Destroying the universe isn't particularly new, skip-wise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you are destroying the universe for what? Because you can?

it's not up to you to end the universe that so many other authors are working on to give more impact to your story.

Too much of this story feels like you want to claim the SCP universe as your own.

Lol.

It is one story.

Take a seat, because what I'm about to say may come as a shock.

There are hundreds of stories on this website thay have life/the universe/reality being desyroyed.

Some of them, actual multiple entries, are SCP files. There are actually 2 (two!) SCP-001s that end the world. Wild right?

That's right. You heard it first here. It is okay for an SCP article to end the world! It has happened before, several times, in fact, and it will happen again.

The damndest thing about it? Life goes on. People keep writing. One story of universal destruction isn't suddenly going to lock 1,2000+ other people out of submitting in the future.

There is no canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not sure how to feel about this. While well written enough, it basically feels like Saints Row 4, you are destroying the universe for what? Because you can?

You have done good work, but it's not up to you to end the universe that so many other authors are working on to give more impact to your story. Everything is going to bot in your story to the point nothing else really matters anymore. Too much of this story feels like you want to claim the SCP universe as your own.

I remember something said in an article about the ethics council. How the SCP kept treating like everything was the end of the world, but the world just kept going. This article throws that all out the window.

I hate to say it, but this is the first article I have to give a down vote too, and not for writing quality but for content quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay for a hub! I’m curious how if at all Ortothan interacts with the other church GOIs. Actually, I bet ORIA has run across them given how old the church appears to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It exists in some form at the new RCP, and I've relived the experience by listening to Eastside Steve's reading of it, which, as a derivative work, is legally allowed to retain its content as the Eastside YouTube channel is the rightful owner of that intellectual property.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's patently against our site rules.

We're not going to retain an author's work if they choose to delete it. Authors have the right to delete their work at any time. Von Pincier took their work to another location, and thus it would be incredibly wrong of us to use their work in our content archives, like that.

But it's very depressing to me how most of our userbase is OK with casually erasing our site history and iconic articles with no evidence that they ever existed originally.

We did not do this. The author did. Author autonomy is sacrosanct 'round here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, I thoroughly agree; I've mentioned before that I'd prefer authors not actually rewrite their older articles because even if they don't meet the standards we have now, maintaining a sense of history is important (that, and as I've mentioned before, when you engage in collaborative writing, you're also collaborating with the community's past — and for that to work, the past needs to be a static place).

That being said, this is a weirdly specific exception: The author deleted their work. As distasteful as I find that decision, rewriting his work (or just re-copying it) would have been even more distasteful. We should respect his decision and, as a community, not link to the stories he doesn't want us to have.

The only reason this article is here is because 9Volt just happened to have an article on-hand that she realized would — with a few minor tweaks — literally be a 'hateful star'. It's like you're looking for a place to mount your painting at an art gallery when Vincent Van Gogh shows up and yanks down his "Starry Night", just leaving the plaque declaring its name… and you suddenly realize, holy shit this is hilarious, my painting is literally of a starry night, I have to put it up here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Internet Archive links (not in this slot, obviously) in the Tales that use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm curious what you think should have been done instead, since Von Pincier deleted the article of their own volition. We don't retain articles that the author deleted without that author's permission (at least there are no cases of this that I know of), so what else would we do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have anything against 9Volt for making this article, and it looks like a well-written piece of work. But it's very depressing to me how most of our userbase is OK with casually erasing our site history and iconic articles with no evidence that they ever existed originally. It's extremely naive and short-sighted, and something that I think future literary historians will judge us very harshly for.

Not to sound like a crotchety old man, but most of our userbase (myself included) is quite young and seems to lack perspective on just how culturally important this site is and just how much of a disservice we are doing future generations of readers with phenomena like this.

item.title: My possible headcanons
item.content:encoded:
So in this universe, the Pattern Screamers have nearly won. That is, if “the Voruteut” is the Ortothan word describing them.

I wonder… if the sun really just wants to protect itself and not humanity, perhaps even its last resort may not work against the enemy.

I liked this no-rewrite, so +1.

Btw, I translated this into spanish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article was confusing to read through, and I never really had any aha moment. I suppose I need to read the other SCPs/background info for the GoI it seems to be based around in order to understand what's being said here.
